name: HR & Payroll official sources
notes: HR & Payroll carries statutory, legally-binding numbers: employer social-security contribution RATES and the earnings CEILINGS those rates sit on. The rate moves rarely; the ceiling moves often. Kenya's NSSF upper earnings limit has stepped up every February (KES 18,000 -> 36,000 -> 72,000 -> 108,000 by Feb 2026). A stale ceiling silently under-states every employer cost above the old limit.

data/hr/employer-cost-data.js (EMPLOYER_COST_RULES, 54 markets) and data/hr/social-security-rates.js (SOCIAL_SECURITY, 34 markets) are the seed sources. They carry no per-figure date. Treat any ceiling not listed in verifiedCeilings, or older than reviewCadenceDays, as planning-grade.

Never update a rate or a ceiling because a URL's content hash changed. Open the body's published rate schedule or the gazette and read it, exactly as .claude/rules/government.md and .claude/rules/energy.md require.

A social-security body or revenue authority always outranks a payroll-vendor blog or aggregator for the same claim. Vendor pages (e.g. payspace, payecalculator.co.ke) are a corroboration check, never the citation of record.

15 of 54 markets have a bound official body URL today. The other 39 are recorded in gaps.bodiesWithoutUrl as known liabilities, not to-dos. Do not delete one without either finding the source or showing the figure as planning-grade.

The bug class to watch, per .claude/rules/telecom.md: `country.data.field || 0` turns a missing rate into zero, which then wins a cost comparison. The HR engines avoid it — employee-cost-engine errors on an unknown country, hr-engine returns null and the social-security page shows a 'data not available' toast, and per-scheme missing rates default to 0 WITHIN an authored country (the scheme is still listed). Keep those patterns; never let a dropdown offer a market the dataset lacks.

sources.title: PenCom — Nigeria pension regulator
sources.notes: Regulator for the Contributory Pension Scheme (Pension Reform Act 2014). Authoritative for the 10% employer minimum on basic+housing+transport. Read the current PRA guidelines before changing the rate or base.

sources.title: NHIA — Nigeria health insurance
sources.notes: National Health Insurance Authority Act 2022. The employer NHIS rate in the dataset (3.25%) is an assumption for the formal-sector contribution and should be confirmed against current NHIA regulations.

sources.title: NSSF Kenya — contribution rates
sources.notes: NSSF Act 2013. Tier I / Tier II earnings limits step up each February under the phased schedule. As of 1 Feb 2026: LEL KES 9,000 (Tier I, max 540/side), UEL KES 108,000 (Tier II, max 5,940/side incremental; 6,480/side combined). The official portal's rate page can lag; corroborate the effective-Feb figures against a payroll-compliance source and read the gazette.

sources.title: SHA — Kenya SHIF
sources.notes: Social Health Insurance Act 2023. SHIF is 2.75% of gross (employee side), replacing NHIF from Oct 2024. Not an employer cost, so it does not appear in employee-cost; it is a deduction line in social-security.

sources.authority: SSNIT (Social Security and National Insurance Trust)
sources.title: SSNIT — Ghana
sources.notes: National Pensions Act 766. Employer 13% total (Tier 1 to SSNIT, Tier 2 to an approved trustee). Read the current SSNIT contribution schedule before changing the split.

sources.title: NSSF Tanzania
sources.notes: Mandatory 10% employer for private-sector formal employees. Confirm the current rate and any wage band before changing.

sources.title: WCF Tanzania
sources.notes: Workers Compensation Fund employer levy (0.5% private sector). Read the current WCF tariff order.

sources.title: NSSF Uganda
sources.notes: NSSF Act. Employer 10% of gross. Confirm against the current Act and any amendments.

sources.title: RSSB — Rwanda
sources.notes: Pension employer share rose 3% -> 6% from Jan 2025 (phasing to higher rates). Plus maternity 0.3% and occupational hazards 2%. Re-read the RSSB rate schedule for the current pension percentage — it is on a multi-year escalation.

sources.title: South Africa — UIF
sources.notes: UIF is 1% employer on remuneration capped at a monthly ceiling (the dataset uses R17,712). The UIF earnings ceiling is revised periodically by the Minister — confirm the current cap before trusting it.

sources.title: SARS — Skills Development Levy
sources.notes: SDL is 1% of payroll, employers with annual payroll above the R500,000 threshold. Confirm the threshold and rate in the current SARS guide.

sources.title: NAPSA — Zambia
sources.notes: NAPSA employer 5% up to a monthly ceiling. The ceiling is indexed to national average earnings and is revised annually in January — the dataset value (ZMW 28,800) must be re-read against the current NAPSA notice.

sources.title: CNSS — Morocco
sources.notes: CNSS employer contributions on a capped base (dataset uses MAD 6,000). Confirm the current plafond and the composite employer rate.

sources.title: CNSS — Tunisia
sources.notes: CNSS employer rate (dataset uses 16.57%). Confirm against the current CNSS schedule.

sources.title: CNPS — Côte d'Ivoire
sources.notes: CNPS pension is capped (dataset uses XOF 2,700,000/month); family allowances and work-accident are on gross. The pension ceiling is a multiple of the SMIG and moves when the SMIG moves — confirm it.

sources.title: CNPS — Cameroon
sources.notes: CNPS employer pension, family allowances and work-accident. Confirm the capped base and rates in the current CNPS schedule.

sources.authority: IPRES (Institution de Prévoyance Retraite du Sénégal)
sources.title: IPRES — Senegal
sources.notes: IPRES pension employer share (dataset uses 8.4%). CSS covers health/accidents separately. Confirm both against current schedules.

sources.title: MRA — CSG / NSF / Training Levy
sources.notes: CSG (Contribution Sociale Généralisée), NSF and the training levy are collected by the MRA. Confirm the current employer rates and any wage thresholds.

verifiedCeilings.source: payspace.com NSSF 2026 update, corroborated by payecalculator.co.ke, winguapps.co.ke, smarthr.co.ke and quartet-co.com; NSSF Act 2013 phased schedule. Official nssf.or.ke rate page still served the 2014 base gazette at time of check.

verifiedCeilings.value: KES 108,000/month; 6% each side on the 9,000-108,000 band; max KES 5,940/side (6,480/side combined)
verifiedCeilings.source: payspace.com NSSF 2026 update, corroborated by payecalculator.co.ke, winguapps.co.ke, smarthr.co.ke and quartet-co.com; NSSF Act 2013 phased schedule.

BDO Global and Mercans statutory alerts on the 1 Jan 2026 min/max insurable-wage increase; EgyptToday. Prior cap EGP 12,600 (2024) was stale in the dataset and was corrected to 16,700 in employer-cost-data.js and social-security-rates.js.
Confirmed unchanged since 1 June 2021 across TaxTim, ClearComply and SARS UIF guidance for 2026. Dataset value R17,712 is current — no change needed.

bodiesWithoutUrl.authority: POESSA / Private Organizations Employees Social Security Agency
No functioning national social security system
NASSIT (National Social Security and Insurance Trust)
SSHFC (Social Security and Housing Finance Corporation)

unsourcedClaims.field: Work-accident / workers-compensation employer rates given as an 'average'
unsourcedClaims.why: Several markets (ZA COIDA '1.5% average', NA '3% average', CI/CM/SN work-accident '3% average') use a single placeholder rate where the real rate is industry-risk-banded (e.g. COIDA 0.11-8.26%). These are planning assumptions, not statutory facts, and must be shown as such — never as the employer's actual rate.

unsourcedClaims.field: Statutory earnings ceilings not in verifiedCeilings (ZM NAPSA ZMW 28,800; CI CNPS XOF 2,700,000; MA CNSS MAD 6,000)
unsourcedClaims.why: These caps are indexed (to inflation, national average earnings, or the SMIG) and revised on their own cadence. They were carried over from the seed dataset and have NOT been re-read against the body's current notice this pass. Treat as planning-grade until moved into verifiedCeilings with a date and source. (KE NSSF, EG NOSI and ZA UIF ceilings were verified 2026-07-17 and moved to verifiedCeilings.)
Marked non-mandatory in the dataset ('not legally mandatory but practically universal'). It is a market convention, not a statutory rate, and must never be presented as a legal obligation.
